value.pattern: Epic descriptions that include scope (in/out), measurable outcomes, and dependencies reduce reviewer back-and-forth.
Features that explicitly list functional requirements and non-functional requirements are easier to estimate and split into PBIs.
PBIs with 2+ Given/When/Then acceptance criteria are more testable and produce better outcomes in implementation and QA.
Avoid placeholder language (TBD/TODO) and generic titles like 'update' or 'new feature'. Use specific verbs and measurable outcomes.
If a feature omits security/observability expectations, teams implement inconsistently. Always specify logging, telemetry, and failure behavior.
Include constraints like 'no secrets in code' and 'least privilege RBAC' to align delivery with enterprise governance from day one.
Features that specify required logs/metrics/traces upfront are delivered with fewer production surprises and faster incident resolution.
PBIs that include an explicit DoD checklist (tests, telemetry, docs) have higher completion quality and fewer reopen cycles.
Epics without clear out-of-scope boundaries tend to expand. Always include explicit non-goals or out-of-scope bullets.
If acceptance criteria are not testable, delivery slows. Rewrite AC to be observable, measurable, and verifiable.
PBIs should state failure behavior (timeouts, retries, user messaging). Missing failure modes leads to inconsistent resilience.
